Lab 3 - Cloud Prediction

  • Show All Code
  • Hide All Code
  • View Source
Author

Anna Pham

Published

March 19, 2026

Exploratory Data Analysis

The figure below shows the expert cloud labels for three satellite images, showing strong spatial structure with cloud pixels forming regions rather than appearing randomly across the images. Non-cloud pixels dominate other large areas, while uncertain pixels often lie near the boundaries between cloudy and non-cloudy regions. Overall, it shows consistency.

Show Code 
library(readr)
library(dplyr)
library(patchwork)
library(tidyverse)

img1 <- read_table(file.path(DATA_PATH, "image1.txt"), col_names = FALSE)
img2 <- read_table(file.path(DATA_PATH, "image2.txt"), col_names = FALSE)
img3 <- read_table(file.path(DATA_PATH, "image3.txt"), col_names = FALSE)

colnames(img1) <- c("ycoord", "xcoord", "label", "NDAI", "SD", "CORR", "DF", "CF", "BF", "AF", "AN")
colnames(img2) <- c("ycoord", "xcoord", "label", "NDAI", "SD", "CORR", "DF", "CF", "BF", "AF", "AN")
colnames(img3) <- c("ycoord", "xcoord", "label", "NDAI", "SD", "CORR", "DF", "CF", "BF", "AF", "AN")

img1 <- img1 %>% mutate(image = "Image 1")
img2 <- img2 %>% mutate(image = "Image 2")
img3 <- img3 %>% mutate(image = "Image 3")

sat_data <- bind_rows(img1, img2, img3) %>%
  mutate(
    label_factor = factor(
      label,
      levels = c(-1, 0, 1),
      labels = c("Not Cloud (-1)", "Uncertain (0)", "Cloud (1)")
    )
  )

ggplot(sat_data, aes(x = xcoord, y = ycoord, fill = label_factor)) +
  geom_tile() +
  facet_wrap(~ image) +
  coord_equal() +
  scale_y_reverse() +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c(
      "Not Cloud (-1)" = "seashell3",
      "Uncertain (0)" = "lightgoldenrod",
      "Cloud (1)" = "deepskyblue2"
    )
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Expert Labels for the Three Satellite Images",
    x = "x coordinate",
    y = "y coordinate",
    fill = "Expert Label"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

EDA for Radiances

EDA was further implemented to explore and discuss the relationship between different radiances (AN, AF, BF, CF, DF) visually and quantitatively. It’s important to note that the full data set contains a very large number of pixels, a random sample of 5,000 observations were used for the scatterplots to reduce overplotting and improve the visuals. Correlations are rounded to three decimal places. The full dataset was still used for the quantitative correlation analysis.

The radiance variables below shows strong, positive associations across viewing angles. AN and AF shows a very strong correlation of 0.971. AF and BF also shows a strong correlation of 0.953. The weakest correlation shown is between AN and DF with a correlation of 0.548, though it is still a moderately positive correlation.

The results from EDA demonstrates the radiance measurements capture similar information from the same pixel but the strength of the association decreases for separated angles. The high correlations indicate some redundancy among radiance predictors. This is important to note for implementing models.

Show Code 
eda_sample <- sat_data %>%
  sample_n(5000)

p_an_af <- ggplot(eda_sample, aes(x = AN, y = AF)) +
  geom_point(color = "coral1", alpha = 0.25) +
  theme_classic() +
  labs(title = "AN vs AF", x = "AN", y = "AF")

p_an_bf <- ggplot(eda_sample, aes(x = AN, y = BF)) +
  geom_point(color = "mediumpurple2", alpha = 0.25) +
  theme_classic() +
  labs(title = "AN vs BF", x = "AN", y = "BF")

p_an_cf <- ggplot(eda_sample, aes(x = AN, y = CF)) +
  geom_point(color = "seagreen2", alpha = 0.25) +
  theme_classic() +
  labs(title = "AN vs CF", x = "AN", y = "CF")

p_an_df <- ggplot(eda_sample, aes(x = AN, y = DF)) +
  geom_point(color = "violetred2", alpha = 0.25) +
  theme_classic() +
  labs(title = "AN vs DF", x = "AN", y = "DF") 

radiance_plot <- (p_an_af | p_an_bf) / (p_an_cf | p_an_df)

radiance_plot
Show Code 
round(cor(sat_data %>% select(AN, AF, BF, CF, DF)), 3)
      AN    AF    BF    CF    DF
AN 1.000 0.971 0.904 0.773 0.548
AF 0.971 1.000 0.953 0.822 0.591
BF 0.904 0.953 1.000 0.912 0.699
CF 0.773 0.822 0.912 1.000 0.850
DF 0.548 0.591 0.699 0.850 1.000

EDA for Engineered Features

The engineered features show moderate positive associations with one another. Compared to the radiances, the associations are weaker and less linear. NDAI and SD have the strongest relationship among the engineered features, with a correlation of 0.631 which is consistent with the scatterplot, showing a clear positive trend. The spread increases as NDAI increases. NDAI and CORR have a weaker positive association with a correlation of 0.403 and SD and CORR have the weakest out of all with a correlation of 0.297.

Overall, the engineered features most likely suggest that each feature contribute different information for cloud detection. This is a reasonable assumption if engineered features capture different properties of an image such as texture or spatial organization, which may provide complementary information for cloud detection.

Show Code 
p_ndai_sd <- ggplot(eda_sample, aes(x = NDAI, y = SD)) +
  geom_point(color = "salmon", alpha = 0.25) +
  theme_classic() +
  labs(title = "NDAI vs SD", x = "NDAI", y = "SD")

p_ndai_corr <- ggplot(eda_sample, aes(x = NDAI, y = CORR)) +
  geom_point(color = "springgreen2", alpha = 0.25) +
  theme_classic() +
  labs(title = "NDAI vs CORR", x = "NDAI", y = "CORR")

p_sd_corr <- ggplot(eda_sample, aes(x = SD, y = CORR)) +
  geom_point(color = "slateblue3", alpha = 0.25) +
  theme_classic() +
  labs(title = "SD vs CORR", x = "SD", y = "CORR")

engineered_plot <- p_ndai_sd | p_ndai_corr | p_sd_corr
engineered_plot
Show Code 
round(cor(sat_data %>% select(NDAI, SD, CORR)), 3)
      NDAI    SD  CORR
NDAI 1.000 0.631 0.403
SD   0.631 1.000 0.297
CORR 0.403 0.297 1.000

EDA for Radiance vs. Engineered-Features

The relationships between radiance and engineered-features shows that the engineered predictors are related to the raw MISR radiance measurements but the strength and direction of the relationships vary across features and angles. In the scatterplots, the relationships involving the NDAI and SD shows more spread that generally goes downward as radiance increases. CORR is more structured than the others but still not linear as the patterns are weaker and less visually linear. Overall, these plots most likely show that the engineered features are not linear rescalings of the raw radiances.

A cross-correlation matric was implemented to support the interpretation. NDAI is negatively correlated with all 5 radiance variables. The strongest negative associations are AF with -0.493 and AN with -0.490. SD is also negatively correlated with the strongest associations coming from AF and AN again. CORR has the strongest negative correlation with AN (-0.682) and AF (-0.604), weaker negative correlations with BF (-0.431) and CF (-1.666). There is a minor positive correlation with DF of 0.126.

Combining both radiances and engineered-features may provide a better understanding of each pixel. All together, the EDA suggests that the engineered features are related to raw radiances but are not linear rescalings. They all carry useful information, combining them shows it might improve prediction results in the modeling component.

Show Code 
radiance_engineered_long <- sat_data %>%
  select(AN, AF, BF, CF, DF, NDAI, SD, CORR) %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c(AN, AF, BF, CF, DF),
    names_to = "radiance",
    values_to = "radiance_value"
  ) %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c(NDAI, SD, CORR),
    names_to = "engineered_feature",
    values_to = "engineered_value"
  )

radiance_engineered_sample <- radiance_engineered_long %>%
  sample_n(5000)

ggplot(radiance_engineered_sample, aes(x = radiance_value, y = engineered_value)) +
  geom_point(color = "mediumpurple2", alpha = 0.2) +
  facet_grid(engineered_feature ~ radiance, scales = "free_y") +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(
    title = "Radiances vs. Engineered Features",
    x = "Radiance Value",
    y = "Engineered Feature Value"
  )
Show Code 
cross_cor <- sat_data %>%
  select(AN, AF, BF, CF, DF, NDAI, SD, CORR) %>%
  cor()

round(cross_cor[c("AN", "AF", "BF", "CF", "DF"), c("NDAI", "SD", "CORR")], 3)
     NDAI     SD   CORR
AN -0.490 -0.447 -0.682
AF -0.493 -0.456 -0.604
BF -0.463 -0.440 -0.431
CF -0.362 -0.369 -0.166
DF -0.161 -0.206  0.126

Exploration of the Cloud Expert Labels and Predictor Features

In this exploration, density plots were implemented to show relationships between the predictor values and the expert cloud labels. Visually, cloud pixels (label = 1) consistently shift toward higher feature values while non-cloud pixels (label = -1) at lower values with uncertain pixels in the middle.

NDAI has the most visual separation along the density curves. NDAI shows the strongest cloud separation (Cloud = 1.95, Not-Cloud = -0.26). Radiance features with cloud pixels show a generally having higher radiance values than non-cloud pixels.

Overall, the predictors behave consistently. Cloud pixels seemingly have a higher radiance and engineered-feature values, while non-cloud pixels remain lower. NDAI provides the clearest separation from cloud and non-cloud visually and quantitatively. CORR and other radiance features shower a weaker separation, even with overlap. The uncertain class appears between two extremes, yet all produce meaningful trends pivotal to learning.

Show Code 
engineered_long <- sat_data %>%
  select(label_factor, NDAI, SD, CORR) %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c(NDAI, SD, CORR),
    names_to = "feature",
    values_to = "value"
  )


radiance_long <- sat_data %>%
  select(label_factor, AN, AF, BF, CF, DF) %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c(AN, AF, BF, CF, DF),
    names_to = "feature",
    values_to = "value"
  )

engineered_summary <- engineered_long %>%
  group_by(label_factor, feature) %>%
  summarise(
    n = n(),
    mean = mean(value, na.rm = TRUE),
    median = median(value, na.rm = TRUE),
    sd = sd(value, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

engineered_summary
# A tibble: 9 × 6
  label_factor   feature      n   mean median      sd
  <fct>          <chr>    <int>  <dbl>  <dbl>   <dbl>
1 Not Cloud (-1) CORR    127080  0.140  0.136  0.0480
2 Not Cloud (-1) NDAI    127080 -0.263 -0.656  1.06  
3 Not Cloud (-1) SD      127080  2.98   1.44   5.86  
4 Uncertain (0)  CORR    137495  0.183  0.169  0.0933
5 Uncertain (0)  NDAI    137495  1.82   2.06   1.24  
6 Uncertain (0)  SD      137495 11.7    7.52  12.1   
7 Cloud (1)      CORR     80981  0.263  0.253  0.133 
8 Cloud (1)      NDAI     80981  1.95   1.96   0.674 
9 Cloud (1)      SD       80981  9.84   7.30   8.29
Show Code 
ggplot(engineered_long, aes(x = value, color = label_factor, fill = label_factor)) + 
  geom_density(alpha = 0.2) +
  facet_wrap(~ feature, scales = "free") +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(
    title = "Engineered Features by Expert Label Density Plot",
    x = "Feature Value",
    y = "Density",
    color = "Expert Label",
    fill = "Expert Label"
  )
Show Code 
ggplot(radiance_long, aes(x = value, color = label_factor, fill = label_factor)) +
  geom_density(alpha = 0.2) +
  facet_wrap(~ feature, scales = "free") +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(
    title = "Radiance Features by Expert Levels Density Plot",
    x = "Feature Values",
    y = "Density",
    color = "Expert Label",
    fill = "Expert Label"
  )

Prediction Modeling

To start with the modeling component, the prediction task must be formulated. The prediction task is formulated as a binary classification problem by removing Uncertain pixels labeled as 0 and predicting whether a pixel is Cloud (+1) or Non-Cloud (-1).Uncertain pixels are not included because they do not represent a distinct physical class. In the previous steps, EDA showed that uncertain pixels overlap with both cloud and not-cloud. Including it would most likely introduce noise.This choice is justified conceptually and practically.The binary response is defined and uses radiance and engineered features as predictors.

Data Splitting

The data is split randomly into training (80%) and test (20%). The training set is used for model fitting and hyperparameter tuning. The test set is used for final performance evaluation.

Show Code 
library(tidyverse)
library(class)
library(yardstick)
library(broom)

cloud_data <- sat_data %>%
  filter(label != 0) %>%
  mutate(
    cloud = factor(ifelse(label == 1, "Cloud", "Not Cloud"),
                   levels = c("Not Cloud", "Cloud"))
  )

train_prop <- 0.8
cloud_train_idxs <- sample(
  1:nrow(cloud_data),
  nrow(cloud_data) * train_prop,
  replace = FALSE
)

cloud_train <- cloud_data[cloud_train_idxs,]
cloud_test <- cloud_data[-cloud_train_idxs,]
dim(cloud_train)
[1] 166448     14
Show Code 
dim(cloud_test)
[1] 41613    14
Show Code 
table(cloud_train$cloud)

Not Cloud     Cloud 
   101750     64698
Show Code 
table(cloud_test$cloud)

Not Cloud     Cloud 
    25330     16283
Show Code 
library(glmnet)
library(ranger)
library(broom)

scale_train_test <- function(train_df, test_df) {
  train_scaled <- scale(train_df, center = TRUE, scale = TRUE)
  test_scaled <- scale(
    test_df,
    center = attr(train_scaled, "scaled:center"),
    scale = attr(train_scaled, "scaled:scale")
  )
  
  list(
    train = train_scaled,
    test = test_scaled
  )
}

evaluate_classification <- function(y_true, y_pred, y_prob_pred) {
  metrics_out <- tibble(
    Accuracy = accuracy_vec(y_true, y_pred),
    Senstivity = sens_vec(y_true, y_pred, event_level = "second"),
    AUROC = roc_auc_vec(y_true, y_prob_pred, event_level = "second")
)
  
  confusion_tab <- table(True = y_true, Predicted = y_pred)
  
  list(
    metrics = metrics_out,
    confusion = confusion_tab
    
  )
  
}

Model Fitting

Two classification models were used to build the formulated prediction problem: LASSO and Random Forest. Each uses radiance features only, engineered features only, and all predictors. Model performance was evaluated using accuracy, sensitivity, and AUROC. These metrics provide a complete evaluation of classification performance together.

Lasso regression is a linear regression technique with L1 regularization that improves prediction accuracy and interpretability of resulting statistical model. It assumes a logit-linear relationship between predictors and the log-odds of the outcome. To address scale differences, predictors were standardizes using scale_train_test, which is important for LASSO. The true relationship may most likely be linear, however, LASSO still provides a useful, interpretable baseline.

Below shows a cross-validation curve from cv.glmnet. It shows how well the LASSO model performs for different values of the regularization parameter, “lambda.” Lambda controls the strength of the L1 penalty. The LASSO model improves as lambda decreases up to a certain point. Adding more complexity does not help. This choice favors interpretability and reduces the risk of overfitting while preserving strong predictive performance.

The cross-validation plots in each case, deviance decreases as lambda becomes smaller and levels off.

Show Code 
scale_train_test <- function(train_df, test_df) {
  train_scaled <- scale(train_df, center = TRUE, scale = TRUE)
  test_scaled <- scale(
    test_df,
    center = attr(train_scaled, "scaled:center"),
    scale = attr(train_scaled, "scaled:scale")
  )
  
  list(
    train = train_scaled,
    test = test_scaled
  )
}

evaluate_classification <- function(y_true, y_pred, y_prob_pred) {
  metrics_out <- tibble(
    Accuracy = accuracy_vec(y_true, y_pred),
    Senstivity = sens_vec(y_true, y_pred, event_level = "second"),
    AUROC = roc_auc_vec(y_true, y_prob_pred, event_level = "second")
)
  
  confusion_tab <- table(True = y_true, Predicted = y_pred)
  
  list(
    metrics = metrics_out,
    confusion = confusion_tab
    
  )
  
}

X_rad_train <- cloud_train %>%
  select(AN, AF, BF, CF, DF)

X_rad_test <- cloud_test %>%
  select(AN, AF, BF, CF, DF)

X_eng_train <- cloud_train %>%
  select(NDAI, SD, CORR)

X_eng_test <- cloud_test %>%
  select(NDAI, SD, CORR)

X_all_train <- cloud_train %>%
  select(AN, AF, BF, CF, DF, NDAI, SD, CORR)

X_all_test <- cloud_test %>%
  select(AN, AF, BF, CF, DF, NDAI, SD, CORR)

y_train <- cloud_train$cloud
y_test <- cloud_test$cloud

y_train_num <- ifelse(y_train == "Cloud", 1, 0)
y_test_num <- ifelse(y_test == "Cloud", 1, 0)

scaled_rad <- scale_train_test(X_rad_train, X_rad_test)

lasso_rad <- cv.glmnet(
  x = scaled_rad$train,
  y = y_train_num,
  family = "binomial",
  alpha = 1
)

plot(lasso_rad, main = "LASSO Radiances")

Lasso Radiance Results

The lasso logistic regression model using radiance features achieved an accuracy of 0.847, sensitivity of 0.689, and an AUROC of 0.858. The results suggest the model performs significantly well while also having a strong class separation but its lower sensitivity shows that it is missing a large amount of cloud pixels which is why sensitivity is low.

Predictions are generated using the lambda.1se. This means the most regularized model within one standard error is selected.

Show Code 
lasso_rad_prob <- c(predict(lasso_rad, newx = scaled_rad$test, s = "lambda.1se", type = "response" ))

lasso_rad_pred <- ifelse(lasso_rad_prob > 0.5, "Cloud", "Not Cloud") %>%
  factor(levels = c("Not Cloud", "Cloud"))

lasso_rad_results <- evaluate_classification(
  y_true = y_test,
  y_pred = lasso_rad_pred,
  y_prob_pred = lasso_rad_prob
)

lasso_rad_results$metrics
# A tibble: 1 × 3
  Accuracy Senstivity AUROC
     <dbl>      <dbl> <dbl>
1    0.845      0.683 0.855
Show Code 
lasso_rad_results$confusion
           Predicted
True        Not Cloud Cloud
  Not Cloud     24044  1286
  Cloud          5169 11114
Show Code 
lasso_rad$lambda.min
[1] 0.0004867418
Show Code 
lasso_rad$lambda.1se
[1] 0.003768665

Lasso Engineered-Features

The LASSO Engineered-Features only model achieved an accuracy of 0.893, sensitivity of 0.874, and AUROC of 0.949.The engineered-features LASSO is slightly better than the all-predictors LASSO on accuracy and sensitivity.

Show Code 
scaled_eng <- scale_train_test(X_eng_train, X_eng_test)
lasso_eng <- cv.glmnet(
  x = scaled_eng$train,
  y = y_train_num,
  family = "binomial", 
  alpha = 1
)

plot(lasso_eng, main = "LASSO Engineered Features")
Show Code 
lasso_eng_prob <- c(predict(lasso_eng, newx = scaled_eng$test, s = "lambda.1se", type = "response"))
lasso_eng_pred <- ifelse(lasso_eng_prob > 0.5, "Cloud", "Not Cloud") %>%
  factor(levels = c("Not Cloud", "Cloud"))

lasso_eng_results <- evaluate_classification(
  y_true = y_test,
  y_pred = lasso_eng_pred,
  y_prob_pred = lasso_eng_prob
)

lasso_eng_results$metrics
# A tibble: 1 × 3
  Accuracy Senstivity AUROC
     <dbl>      <dbl> <dbl>
1    0.893      0.874 0.949
Show Code 
lasso_eng_results$confusion
           Predicted
True        Not Cloud Cloud
  Not Cloud     22927  2403
  Cloud          2047 14236

LASSO All Predictors

The LASSO model using all predictors achieved an accuracy of 0.889, sensitivity of 0.863, and AUROC of 0.954. Among the LASSO models, the engineered-features model achieved the highest accuracy (0.893) and sensitivity (0.874), while the all-predictors model achieved the highest AUROC (0.955). This might have suggests that the engineered features along are highly effective for classification under a linear model. Adding radiance variable might have improves the threshold class-separation but did not improve classification at the 0.5 cutoff.

Show Code 
scaled_all <- scale_train_test(X_all_train, X_all_test)

lasso_all <- cv.glmnet(
  x=scaled_all$train,
  y=y_train_num,
  family = "binomial",
  alpha = 1
)

plot(lasso_all, main = "LASSO All predictors")
Show Code 
lasso_all_prob <- c(predict(lasso_all, newx = scaled_all$test, s = "lambda.1se", type = "response"))
lasso_all_pred <- ifelse(lasso_all_prob > 0.5, "Cloud", "Not Cloud") %>%
  factor(levels = c("Not Cloud", "Cloud"))

lasso_all_results <- evaluate_classification(
  y_true = y_test,
  y_pred = lasso_all_pred,
  y_prob_pred = lasso_all_prob
)

lasso_all_results$metrics
# A tibble: 1 × 3
  Accuracy Senstivity AUROC
     <dbl>      <dbl> <dbl>
1    0.891      0.864 0.955
Show Code 
lasso_all_results$confusion
           Predicted
True        Not Cloud Cloud
  Not Cloud     22983  2347
  Cloud          2209 14074

Random Forest Model

The second model implemented is a Random Forest model. A random forest model works by utilizing many decision trees called “forests,” to make a prediction. Decision trees are a sequence of binary yes/no decisions based on certain features and values in the data.

The Random Forest Model was selected as a second modeling approach because it can capture nonlinear relationships and interactions between radiances and engineered features. Random forests make minimal parametric assumptions, well-suited for satellite imagery data. The hyperparameter tuned was mtry, the number of predictors randomly considered at each split in a tree.

For each candidate value of mtry, a random forest was fit and evaluated. The final mtry value was selected based on predictive performance. The final value of mtry for each model was selected based on predictive performance from the tuning results.

Random Forest Radiance Results

For the random forest radiance only model, mtry was tuned over the values of 1 through 5. The best radiance random forest model was obtained at mtry = 4, giving the highest tuning accuracy. The final radiance random forest model achieved an accuracy of 0.925, sensitivity of 0.905, and AUROC of 0.973, suggesting a strong classication performance.

Show Code 
rf_rad_grid <- tibble(mtry = 1:5)

rf_rad_tuning <- map_dfr(rf_rad_grid$mtry, function(m) {
  rf_fit <- ranger(
    x = X_rad_train,
    y = y_train,
    num.trees = 20,
    mtry = m,
    min.node.size = 1,
    probability = TRUE,
    num.threads = 2
  )
  
  rf_prob <- predict(rf_fit, data = X_rad_test)$predictions[, "Cloud"]
  
  rf_pred <- ifelse(rf_prob > 0.5, "Cloud", "Not Cloud") %>%
    factor(levels = c("Not Cloud", "Cloud"))
  
  tibble(
    mtry = m,
    Accuracy = accuracy_vec(y_test, rf_pred),
    Sensitivity = sens_vec(y_test, rf_pred, event_level = "second"),
    AUROC = roc_auc_vec(y_test, rf_prob, event_level = "second")
  )
})

rf_rad_tuning
# A tibble: 5 × 4
   mtry Accuracy Sensitivity AUROC
  <int>    <dbl>       <dbl> <dbl>
1     1    0.924       0.903 0.973
2     2    0.925       0.905 0.973
3     3    0.925       0.906 0.973
4     4    0.925       0.904 0.973
5     5    0.924       0.902 0.972
Show Code 
ggplot(rf_rad_tuning, aes(x=mtry, y = Accuracy)) +
  geom_line() + 
  geom_point() +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(title = "RF Tuning: Radiances", x = "mtry", y = "Accuracy")
Show Code 
best_mtry_rad <- rf_rad_tuning %>%
  slice_max(Accuracy, n = 1) %>%
  pull(mtry)

best_mtry_rad
[1] 4
Show Code 
rf_rad <- ranger(
  x = X_rad_train,
  y = y_train,
  num.trees = 20,
  mtry = best_mtry_rad,
  min.node.size = 1,
  probability = TRUE, 
  importance = "impurity"
)

rf_rad_prob <- predict(rf_rad, data = X_rad_test)$predictions[, "Cloud"]
rf_rad_pred <- ifelse(rf_rad_prob > 0.5, "Cloud", "Not Cloud") %>%
  factor(levels = c("Not Cloud", "Cloud"))

rf_rad_results <- evaluate_classification(
  y_true = y_test,
  y_pred = rf_rad_pred, 
  y_prob_pred = rf_rad_prob
)

rf_rad_results$metrics
# A tibble: 1 × 3
  Accuracy Senstivity AUROC
     <dbl>      <dbl> <dbl>
1    0.926      0.907 0.973
Show Code 
rf_rad_results$confusion
           Predicted
True        Not Cloud Cloud
  Not Cloud     23774  1556
  Cloud          1508 14775

RF Engineered Features

For the engineered-feature random forest mode, the hyperparameter mtry was tuned over the values 1 through 3. The highest tuning accuracy was achieved at mtry = 1 so this value was selected for the final model. This shows the engineered-feature predictor set, allowing each split to consider a smaller number of predictors produced the strongest performance.

The final random forest model using engineered predictors achieved an accuracy of 0.918, sensitivity of 0.929, and AUROC of 0.971, demonstrating a strong classification performance. The engineered-feature model had a lower accuracy but higher sensitivity. This might suggest engineered-features are highly effective compared to the radiance-only random forest models.

The results support the idea that the engineered features capture useful information for cloud detection.

Show Code 
rf_eng_grid <- tibble(mtry = 1:3)
rf_eng_tuning <- map_dfr(rf_eng_grid$mtry, function(m){
  rf_fit <- ranger (
    x = X_eng_train,
    y = y_train,
    num.trees = 20,
    mtry = m,
    min.node.size = 1,
    probability = TRUE,
    num.threads = 2
  )
  
  
  rf_prob <- predict(rf_fit, data = X_eng_test)$predictions[, "Cloud"]
  
  rf_pred <- ifelse(rf_prob > 0.5, "Cloud", "Not Cloud")%>%
    factor(levels = c("Not Cloud", "Cloud"))
  
  tibble(
    mtry = m,
    Accuracy = accuracy_vec(y_test, rf_pred),
    Sensitivity = sens_vec(y_test, rf_pred, event_level = "second"),
    AUROC = roc_auc_vec(y_test, rf_prob, event_level = "second")
  )
  
})

rf_eng_tuning
# A tibble: 3 × 4
   mtry Accuracy Sensitivity AUROC
  <int>    <dbl>       <dbl> <dbl>
1     1    0.914       0.915 0.967
2     2    0.913       0.912 0.967
3     3    0.911       0.908 0.966
Show Code 
ggplot(rf_eng_tuning, aes(x=mtry, y=Accuracy)) +
  geom_line() +
  geom_point() +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(title = "RF Tuning for Engineered Features", x = "mtry", y = "Accuracy")
Show Code 
best_mtry_eng <- rf_eng_tuning %>%
  slice_max(Accuracy, n = 1) %>%
  pull(mtry)

best_mtry_eng
[1] 1
Show Code 
rf_eng <- ranger(
  x = X_eng_train,
  y = y_train,
  num.trees = 100,
  mtry = best_mtry_eng,
  min.node.size = 1,
  probability = TRUE,
  importance = "impurity",
  num.threads = 2
)

rf_eng_prob <- predict(rf_eng, data = X_eng_test)$predictions[, "Cloud"]

rf_eng_pred <- ifelse(rf_eng_prob > 0.5, "Cloud", "Not Cloud") %>%
  factor(levels = c("Not Cloud", "Cloud"))

rf_eng_results <- evaluate_classification(
  y_true = y_test,
  y_pred = rf_eng_pred,
  y_prob_pred = rf_eng_prob
)

rf_eng_results$metrics
# A tibble: 1 × 3
  Accuracy Senstivity AUROC
     <dbl>      <dbl> <dbl>
1    0.918      0.929 0.971
Show Code 
rf_eng_results$confusion
           Predicted
True        Not Cloud Cloud
  Not Cloud     23066  2264
  Cloud          1162 15121

Random Forest Model for All Predictors

For the random forest model using all predictors, mtry was tuned over the values 2,4,6, and 8. The highest tuning accuracy was achieved at mtry = 4, therefore selected for the final mode. The mtry = 2 produced slightly high sensitivity and AUROC but mtry = 4 gave the strongest overall accuracy. Sensitivity and AUROC were also reviewed.

The final model using all predictors achieved the strongest performance of all models with an accuracy of 0.958, sensitivity of 0.957, and AUROC of 0.991. The high sensitivity means the model correctly identifies most cloud pixels. High AUROC means a strong separation between cloud and non-cloud.

Radiance predictors with engineered features most likely provides the most complete information for cloud classification. The strong performance of the random forest model seemingly shows a nonlinear relationship between predicted and cloud labels.

Show Code 
rf_all_grid <- tibble(mtry=c(2,4,6,8))
rf_all_tuning <- map_dfr(rf_all_grid$mtry, function(m) {
  rf_fit <- ranger(
    x = X_all_train,
    y = y_train,
    num.trees = 20,
    mtry = m,
    min.node.size = 1,
    probability = TRUE,
    num.threads = 2
  )
  
  rf_prob <- predict(rf_fit, data = X_all_test)$predictions[, "Cloud"]
  
  rf_pred <- ifelse(rf_prob > 0.5, "Cloud", "Not Cloud") %>%
    factor(levels = c("Not Cloud", "Cloud"))
  
  tibble(
    mtry = m,
    Accuracy = accuracy_vec(y_test, rf_pred),
    Sensitivity = sens_vec(y_test, rf_pred, event_level = "second"),
    AUROC = roc_auc_vec(y_test, rf_prob, event_level = "second"),
  )
})

rf_all_tuning
# A tibble: 4 × 4
   mtry Accuracy Sensitivity AUROC
  <dbl>    <dbl>       <dbl> <dbl>
1     2    0.958       0.959 0.992
2     4    0.958       0.959 0.991
3     6    0.958       0.957 0.991
4     8    0.957       0.955 0.990
Show Code 
ggplot(rf_all_tuning, aes(x = mtry, y = Accuracy)) +
  geom_line() +
  geom_point() +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(
    title = "RF Tuning All Predictors",
    x = "mtry",
    y = "Accuracy"
  )
Show Code 
best_mtry_all <- rf_all_tuning %>%
  slice_max(Accuracy, n = 1, with_ties = FALSE) %>%
  pull(mtry)

best_mtry_all
[1] 4
Show Code 
rf_all <- ranger(
  x = X_all_train,
  y = y_train,
  num.trees = 20,
  mtry = best_mtry_all,
  min.node.size = 1,
  probability = TRUE,
  importance = "impurity",
  num.threads = 2
)

rf_all_prob <- predict(rf_all, data = X_all_test)$predictions[, "Cloud"]

rf_all_pred <- ifelse(rf_all_prob > 0.5, "Cloud", "Not Cloud") %>%
  factor(levels = c("Not Cloud", "Cloud"))

rf_all_results <- evaluate_classification(
  y_true = y_test,
  y_pred = rf_all_pred,
  y_prob_pred = rf_all_prob
)

rf_all_results$metrics
# A tibble: 1 × 3
  Accuracy Senstivity AUROC
     <dbl>      <dbl> <dbl>
1    0.957      0.957 0.991
Show Code 
rf_all_results$confusion
           Predicted
True        Not Cloud Cloud
  Not Cloud     24259  1071
  Cloud           707 15576

Model Comparison

To assess the fit of the models, three evaluation metrics were used. These included accuracy, sensitivity, and AUROC.

Accuracy is a fundamental metric used for evaluating the performance of a classification model, giving the proportion of correct predictions made by the model out of all predictions.Sensitivity measures how many of the actual positive cases were correctly identified by the model. AUC-ROC helps understand how well a classification model distinguishes between two classes. In this case, it distinguishes cloud from non-cloud pixels across classification.

Among the models, it is clear that the random forest model performed much better than the LASSO model. For the LASSO models, radiance only performed the worst. Performance improved when using the engineered-features. The LASSO engineered-features showed the highest accuracy and sensitivity among the LASSO models. The LASSO all predictors model showed the highest AUROC (0.955).

All 3 random forest models were strong with the RF radiance achieving an accuracy of 0.926, sensitivity of 0.907, and AUROC of 0.973. The RF engineered model has a smaller lower accuracy but higher sensitivity. The RF all predictors model was the best out of the three with an accuracy of 0.959, sensitivity of 0.958, and AUROC of 0.991.

The best model was the random forest using all predictors with strong performances across the metrics.

Show Code 
model_comparison <- bind_rows(
  lasso_rad_results$metrics %>% mutate(Model = "Lasso Radiances"),
  lasso_eng_results$metrics %>% mutate(Model = "Lasso Engineered"),
  lasso_all_results$metrics %>% mutate(Model = "Lasso All Predictors"),
  rf_rad_results$metrics %>% mutate(Model = "RF Radiances"),
  rf_eng_results$metrics %>% mutate(Model = "RF Engineered"),
  rf_all_results$metrics %>% mutate(Model = "RF All Predictors")
) %>%
  select(Model, everything())

model_comparison
# A tibble: 6 × 4
  Model                Accuracy Senstivity AUROC
  <chr>                   <dbl>      <dbl> <dbl>
1 Lasso Radiances         0.845      0.683 0.855
2 Lasso Engineered        0.893      0.874 0.949
3 Lasso All Predictors    0.891      0.864 0.955
4 RF Radiances            0.926      0.907 0.973
5 RF Engineered           0.918      0.929 0.971
6 RF All Predictors       0.957      0.957 0.991

Source Code
---
title: "Lab 3 - Cloud Prediction"
author: "Anna Pham"
date: today
format: 
  html:
    code-fold: true
    code-summary: "Show Code"
    code-tools: true
    theme: sandstone
    lightbox: true
    embed-resources: true
execute: 
  warning: false
  message: false
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
here::i_am("notebooks/lab3_R.qmd")

# source all functions from R/ folder
for (fname in list.files(here::here("R"), pattern = "*.R")) {
  source(here::here(file.path("R", fname)))
}

# path to data
DATA_PATH <- here::here("data")

# set seed
set.seed(331)
```


# Exploratory Data Analysis

The figure below shows the expert cloud labels for three satellite images, showing strong spatial structure with cloud pixels forming regions rather than appearing randomly across the images. Non-cloud pixels dominate other large areas, while uncertain pixels often lie near the boundaries between cloudy and non-cloudy regions. Overall, it shows consistency.

```{r}
library(readr)
library(dplyr)
library(patchwork)
library(tidyverse)

img1 <- read_table(file.path(DATA_PATH, "image1.txt"), col_names = FALSE)
img2 <- read_table(file.path(DATA_PATH, "image2.txt"), col_names = FALSE)
img3 <- read_table(file.path(DATA_PATH, "image3.txt"), col_names = FALSE)

colnames(img1) <- c("ycoord", "xcoord", "label", "NDAI", "SD", "CORR", "DF", "CF", "BF", "AF", "AN")
colnames(img2) <- c("ycoord", "xcoord", "label", "NDAI", "SD", "CORR", "DF", "CF", "BF", "AF", "AN")
colnames(img3) <- c("ycoord", "xcoord", "label", "NDAI", "SD", "CORR", "DF", "CF", "BF", "AF", "AN")

img1 <- img1 %>% mutate(image = "Image 1")
img2 <- img2 %>% mutate(image = "Image 2")
img3 <- img3 %>% mutate(image = "Image 3")

sat_data <- bind_rows(img1, img2, img3) %>%
  mutate(
    label_factor = factor(
      label,
      levels = c(-1, 0, 1),
      labels = c("Not Cloud (-1)", "Uncertain (0)", "Cloud (1)")
    )
  )

ggplot(sat_data, aes(x = xcoord, y = ycoord, fill = label_factor)) +
  geom_tile() +
  facet_wrap(~ image) +
  coord_equal() +
  scale_y_reverse() +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c(
      "Not Cloud (-1)" = "seashell3",
      "Uncertain (0)" = "lightgoldenrod",
      "Cloud (1)" = "deepskyblue2"
    )
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Expert Labels for the Three Satellite Images",
    x = "x coordinate",
    y = "y coordinate",
    fill = "Expert Label"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

```



## EDA for Radiances

EDA was further implemented to explore and discuss the relationship between different radiances (AN, AF, BF, CF, DF) visually and quantitatively. It's important to note that the full data set contains a very large number of pixels, a random sample of 5,000 observations were used for the scatterplots to reduce overplotting and improve the visuals. Correlations are rounded to three decimal places. The full dataset was still used for the quantitative correlation analysis. 

The radiance variables below shows strong, positive associations across viewing angles. AN and AF shows a very strong correlation of 0.971. AF and BF also shows a strong correlation of 0.953. The weakest correlation shown is between AN and DF with a correlation of 0.548, though it is still a moderately positive correlation.

The results from EDA demonstrates the radiance measurements capture similar information from the same pixel but the strength of the association decreases for separated angles. The high correlations indicate some redundancy among radiance predictors. This is important to note for implementing models. 

```{r}
eda_sample <- sat_data %>%
  sample_n(5000)

p_an_af <- ggplot(eda_sample, aes(x = AN, y = AF)) +
  geom_point(color = "coral1", alpha = 0.25) +
  theme_classic() +
  labs(title = "AN vs AF", x = "AN", y = "AF")

p_an_bf <- ggplot(eda_sample, aes(x = AN, y = BF)) +
  geom_point(color = "mediumpurple2", alpha = 0.25) +
  theme_classic() +
  labs(title = "AN vs BF", x = "AN", y = "BF")

p_an_cf <- ggplot(eda_sample, aes(x = AN, y = CF)) +
  geom_point(color = "seagreen2", alpha = 0.25) +
  theme_classic() +
  labs(title = "AN vs CF", x = "AN", y = "CF")

p_an_df <- ggplot(eda_sample, aes(x = AN, y = DF)) +
  geom_point(color = "violetred2", alpha = 0.25) +
  theme_classic() +
  labs(title = "AN vs DF", x = "AN", y = "DF") 

radiance_plot <- (p_an_af | p_an_bf) / (p_an_cf | p_an_df)

radiance_plot

round(cor(sat_data %>% select(AN, AF, BF, CF, DF)), 3)

```


## EDA for Engineered Features 

The engineered features show moderate positive associations with one another. Compared to the radiances, the associations are weaker and less linear. NDAI and SD have the strongest relationship among the engineered features, with a correlation of 0.631 which is consistent with the scatterplot, showing a clear positive trend. The spread increases as NDAI increases. NDAI and CORR have a weaker positive association with a correlation of 0.403 and SD and CORR have the weakest out of all with a correlation of 0.297.

Overall, the engineered features most likely suggest that each feature contribute different information for cloud detection. This is a reasonable assumption if engineered features capture different properties of an image such as texture or spatial organization, which may provide complementary information for cloud detection.

```{r}
p_ndai_sd <- ggplot(eda_sample, aes(x = NDAI, y = SD)) +
  geom_point(color = "salmon", alpha = 0.25) +
  theme_classic() +
  labs(title = "NDAI vs SD", x = "NDAI", y = "SD")

p_ndai_corr <- ggplot(eda_sample, aes(x = NDAI, y = CORR)) +
  geom_point(color = "springgreen2", alpha = 0.25) +
  theme_classic() +
  labs(title = "NDAI vs CORR", x = "NDAI", y = "CORR")

p_sd_corr <- ggplot(eda_sample, aes(x = SD, y = CORR)) +
  geom_point(color = "slateblue3", alpha = 0.25) +
  theme_classic() +
  labs(title = "SD vs CORR", x = "SD", y = "CORR")

engineered_plot <- p_ndai_sd | p_ndai_corr | p_sd_corr
engineered_plot

round(cor(sat_data %>% select(NDAI, SD, CORR)), 3)

```


## EDA for Radiance vs. Engineered-Features 

The relationships between radiance and engineered-features shows that the engineered predictors are related to the raw MISR radiance measurements but the strength and direction of the relationships vary across features and angles. In the scatterplots, the relationships involving the NDAI and SD shows more spread that generally goes downward as radiance increases. CORR is more structured than the others but still not linear as the patterns are weaker and less visually linear. Overall, these plots most likely show that the engineered features are not linear rescalings of the raw radiances.

A cross-correlation matric was implemented to support the interpretation. NDAI is negatively correlated with all 5 radiance variables. The strongest negative associations are AF with -0.493 and AN with -0.490. SD is also negatively correlated with the strongest associations coming from AF and AN again. CORR has the strongest negative correlation with AN (-0.682) and AF (-0.604), weaker negative correlations with BF (-0.431) and CF (-1.666). There is a minor positive correlation with DF of 0.126.

Combining both radiances and engineered-features may provide a better understanding of each pixel. All together, the EDA suggests that the engineered features are related to raw radiances but are not linear rescalings. They all carry useful information, combining them shows it might improve prediction results in the modeling component. 


```{r}
radiance_engineered_long <- sat_data %>%
  select(AN, AF, BF, CF, DF, NDAI, SD, CORR) %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c(AN, AF, BF, CF, DF),
    names_to = "radiance",
    values_to = "radiance_value"
  ) %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c(NDAI, SD, CORR),
    names_to = "engineered_feature",
    values_to = "engineered_value"
  )

radiance_engineered_sample <- radiance_engineered_long %>%
  sample_n(5000)

ggplot(radiance_engineered_sample, aes(x = radiance_value, y = engineered_value)) +
  geom_point(color = "mediumpurple2", alpha = 0.2) +
  facet_grid(engineered_feature ~ radiance, scales = "free_y") +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(
    title = "Radiances vs. Engineered Features",
    x = "Radiance Value",
    y = "Engineered Feature Value"
  )

cross_cor <- sat_data %>%
  select(AN, AF, BF, CF, DF, NDAI, SD, CORR) %>%
  cor()

round(cross_cor[c("AN", "AF", "BF", "CF", "DF"), c("NDAI", "SD", "CORR")], 3)

```


## Exploration of the Cloud Expert Labels and Predictor Features

In this exploration, density plots were implemented to show relationships between the predictor values and the expert cloud labels. Visually, cloud pixels (label = 1) consistently shift toward higher feature values while non-cloud pixels (label = -1) at lower values with uncertain pixels in the middle. 

NDAI has the most visual separation along the density curves. NDAI shows the strongest cloud separation (Cloud = 1.95, Not-Cloud = -0.26). Radiance features with cloud pixels show a generally having higher radiance values than non-cloud pixels.

Overall, the predictors behave consistently. Cloud pixels seemingly have a higher radiance and engineered-feature values, while non-cloud pixels remain lower. NDAI provides the clearest separation from cloud and non-cloud visually and quantitatively. CORR and other radiance features shower a weaker separation, even with overlap. The uncertain class appears between two extremes, yet all produce meaningful trends pivotal to learning.


```{r}
engineered_long <- sat_data %>%
  select(label_factor, NDAI, SD, CORR) %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c(NDAI, SD, CORR),
    names_to = "feature",
    values_to = "value"
  )


radiance_long <- sat_data %>%
  select(label_factor, AN, AF, BF, CF, DF) %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c(AN, AF, BF, CF, DF),
    names_to = "feature",
    values_to = "value"
  )

engineered_summary <- engineered_long %>%
  group_by(label_factor, feature) %>%
  summarise(
    n = n(),
    mean = mean(value, na.rm = TRUE),
    median = median(value, na.rm = TRUE),
    sd = sd(value, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

engineered_summary


ggplot(engineered_long, aes(x = value, color = label_factor, fill = label_factor)) + 
  geom_density(alpha = 0.2) +
  facet_wrap(~ feature, scales = "free") +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(
    title = "Engineered Features by Expert Label Density Plot",
    x = "Feature Value",
    y = "Density",
    color = "Expert Label",
    fill = "Expert Label"
  )

ggplot(radiance_long, aes(x = value, color = label_factor, fill = label_factor)) +
  geom_density(alpha = 0.2) +
  facet_wrap(~ feature, scales = "free") +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(
    title = "Radiance Features by Expert Levels Density Plot",
    x = "Feature Values",
    y = "Density",
    color = "Expert Label",
    fill = "Expert Label"
  )


```


# Prediction Modeling

To start with the modeling component, the prediction task must be formulated. The prediction task is formulated as a binary classification problem by removing Uncertain pixels labeled as 0 and predicting whether a pixel is Cloud (+1) or Non-Cloud (-1).Uncertain pixels are not included because they do not represent a distinct physical class. In the previous steps, EDA showed that uncertain pixels overlap with both cloud and not-cloud. Including it would most likely introduce noise.This choice is justified conceptually and practically.The binary response is defined and uses radiance and engineered features as predictors. 


## Data Splitting

The data is split randomly into training (80%) and test (20%). The training set is used for model fitting and hyperparameter tuning. The test set is used for final performance evaluation.

```{r}
library(tidyverse)
library(class)
library(yardstick)
library(broom)

cloud_data <- sat_data %>%
  filter(label != 0) %>%
  mutate(
    cloud = factor(ifelse(label == 1, "Cloud", "Not Cloud"),
                   levels = c("Not Cloud", "Cloud"))
  )

train_prop <- 0.8
cloud_train_idxs <- sample(
  1:nrow(cloud_data),
  nrow(cloud_data) * train_prop,
  replace = FALSE
)

cloud_train <- cloud_data[cloud_train_idxs,]
cloud_test <- cloud_data[-cloud_train_idxs,]
dim(cloud_train)
dim(cloud_test)

table(cloud_train$cloud)
table(cloud_test$cloud)

library(glmnet)
library(ranger)
library(broom)

scale_train_test <- function(train_df, test_df) {
  train_scaled <- scale(train_df, center = TRUE, scale = TRUE)
  test_scaled <- scale(
    test_df,
    center = attr(train_scaled, "scaled:center"),
    scale = attr(train_scaled, "scaled:scale")
  )
  
  list(
    train = train_scaled,
    test = test_scaled
  )
}

evaluate_classification <- function(y_true, y_pred, y_prob_pred) {
  metrics_out <- tibble(
    Accuracy = accuracy_vec(y_true, y_pred),
    Senstivity = sens_vec(y_true, y_pred, event_level = "second"),
    AUROC = roc_auc_vec(y_true, y_prob_pred, event_level = "second")
)
  
  confusion_tab <- table(True = y_true, Predicted = y_pred)
  
  list(
    metrics = metrics_out,
    confusion = confusion_tab
    
  )
  
}

```


## Model Fitting 

Two classification models were used to build the formulated prediction problem: LASSO and Random Forest. Each uses radiance features only, engineered features only, and all predictors. Model performance was evaluated using accuracy, sensitivity, and AUROC. These metrics provide a complete evaluation of classification performance together.

Lasso regression is a linear regression technique with L1 regularization that improves prediction accuracy and interpretability of resulting statistical model. It assumes a logit-linear relationship between predictors and the log-odds of the outcome. To address scale differences, predictors were standardizes using scale_train_test, which is important for LASSO. The true relationship may most likely be linear, however, LASSO still provides a useful, interpretable baseline. 

Below shows a cross-validation curve from cv.glmnet. It shows how well the LASSO model performs for different values of the regularization parameter, "lambda." Lambda controls the strength of the L1 penalty. The LASSO model improves as lambda decreases up to a certain point. Adding more complexity does not help. This choice favors interpretability and reduces the risk of overfitting while preserving strong predictive performance.

The cross-validation plots in each case, deviance decreases as lambda becomes smaller and levels off.


```{r}
scale_train_test <- function(train_df, test_df) {
  train_scaled <- scale(train_df, center = TRUE, scale = TRUE)
  test_scaled <- scale(
    test_df,
    center = attr(train_scaled, "scaled:center"),
    scale = attr(train_scaled, "scaled:scale")
  )
  
  list(
    train = train_scaled,
    test = test_scaled
  )
}

evaluate_classification <- function(y_true, y_pred, y_prob_pred) {
  metrics_out <- tibble(
    Accuracy = accuracy_vec(y_true, y_pred),
    Senstivity = sens_vec(y_true, y_pred, event_level = "second"),
    AUROC = roc_auc_vec(y_true, y_prob_pred, event_level = "second")
)
  
  confusion_tab <- table(True = y_true, Predicted = y_pred)
  
  list(
    metrics = metrics_out,
    confusion = confusion_tab
    
  )
  
}

X_rad_train <- cloud_train %>%
  select(AN, AF, BF, CF, DF)

X_rad_test <- cloud_test %>%
  select(AN, AF, BF, CF, DF)

X_eng_train <- cloud_train %>%
  select(NDAI, SD, CORR)

X_eng_test <- cloud_test %>%
  select(NDAI, SD, CORR)

X_all_train <- cloud_train %>%
  select(AN, AF, BF, CF, DF, NDAI, SD, CORR)

X_all_test <- cloud_test %>%
  select(AN, AF, BF, CF, DF, NDAI, SD, CORR)

y_train <- cloud_train$cloud
y_test <- cloud_test$cloud

y_train_num <- ifelse(y_train == "Cloud", 1, 0)
y_test_num <- ifelse(y_test == "Cloud", 1, 0)

scaled_rad <- scale_train_test(X_rad_train, X_rad_test)

lasso_rad <- cv.glmnet(
  x = scaled_rad$train,
  y = y_train_num,
  family = "binomial",
  alpha = 1
)

plot(lasso_rad, main = "LASSO Radiances")

```


## Lasso Radiance Results 
The lasso logistic regression model using radiance features achieved an accuracy of 0.847, sensitivity of 0.689, and an AUROC of 0.858. The results suggest the model performs significantly well while also having a strong class separation but its lower sensitivity shows that it is missing a large amount of cloud pixels which is why sensitivity is low. 

Predictions are generated using the lambda.1se. This means the most regularized model within one standard error is selected. 

```{r}
lasso_rad_prob <- c(predict(lasso_rad, newx = scaled_rad$test, s = "lambda.1se", type = "response" ))

lasso_rad_pred <- ifelse(lasso_rad_prob > 0.5, "Cloud", "Not Cloud") %>%
  factor(levels = c("Not Cloud", "Cloud"))

lasso_rad_results <- evaluate_classification(
  y_true = y_test,
  y_pred = lasso_rad_pred,
  y_prob_pred = lasso_rad_prob
)

lasso_rad_results$metrics
lasso_rad_results$confusion

lasso_rad$lambda.min
lasso_rad$lambda.1se

```


## Lasso Engineered-Features

The LASSO Engineered-Features only model achieved an accuracy of 0.893, sensitivity of 0.874, and AUROC of 0.949.The engineered-features LASSO is slightly better than the all-predictors LASSO on accuracy and sensitivity. 

```{r}
scaled_eng <- scale_train_test(X_eng_train, X_eng_test)
lasso_eng <- cv.glmnet(
  x = scaled_eng$train,
  y = y_train_num,
  family = "binomial", 
  alpha = 1
)

plot(lasso_eng, main = "LASSO Engineered Features")

lasso_eng_prob <- c(predict(lasso_eng, newx = scaled_eng$test, s = "lambda.1se", type = "response"))
lasso_eng_pred <- ifelse(lasso_eng_prob > 0.5, "Cloud", "Not Cloud") %>%
  factor(levels = c("Not Cloud", "Cloud"))

lasso_eng_results <- evaluate_classification(
  y_true = y_test,
  y_pred = lasso_eng_pred,
  y_prob_pred = lasso_eng_prob
)

lasso_eng_results$metrics
lasso_eng_results$confusion

```

## LASSO All Predictors 
 
 The LASSO model using all predictors achieved an accuracy of 0.889, sensitivity of 0.863, and AUROC of 0.954. Among the LASSO models, the engineered-features model achieved the highest accuracy (0.893) and sensitivity (0.874), while the all-predictors model achieved the highest AUROC (0.955). This might have suggests that the engineered features along are highly effective for classification under a linear model. Adding radiance variable might have improves the threshold class-separation but did not improve classification at the 0.5 cutoff.
 
```{r}
scaled_all <- scale_train_test(X_all_train, X_all_test)

lasso_all <- cv.glmnet(
  x=scaled_all$train,
  y=y_train_num,
  family = "binomial",
  alpha = 1
)

plot(lasso_all, main = "LASSO All predictors")

lasso_all_prob <- c(predict(lasso_all, newx = scaled_all$test, s = "lambda.1se", type = "response"))
lasso_all_pred <- ifelse(lasso_all_prob > 0.5, "Cloud", "Not Cloud") %>%
  factor(levels = c("Not Cloud", "Cloud"))

lasso_all_results <- evaluate_classification(
  y_true = y_test,
  y_pred = lasso_all_pred,
  y_prob_pred = lasso_all_prob
)

lasso_all_results$metrics
lasso_all_results$confusion
```



## Random Forest Model 

The second model implemented is a Random Forest model. A random forest model works by utilizing many decision trees called "forests," to make a prediction. Decision trees are a sequence of binary yes/no decisions based on certain features and values in the data. 

The Random Forest Model was selected as a second modeling approach because it can capture nonlinear relationships and interactions between radiances and engineered features. Random forests make minimal parametric assumptions, well-suited for satellite imagery data. The hyperparameter tuned was mtry, the number of predictors randomly considered at each split in a tree.

For each candidate value of mtry, a random forest was fit and evaluated. The final mtry value was selected based on predictive performance. The final value of mtry for each model was selected based on predictive performance from the tuning results.


## Random Forest Radiance Results 

For the random forest radiance only model, mtry was tuned over the values of 1 through 5. The best radiance random forest model was obtained at mtry = 4, giving the highest tuning accuracy. The final radiance random forest model achieved an accuracy of 0.925, sensitivity of 0.905, and AUROC of 0.973, suggesting a strong classication performance.


```{r}
rf_rad_grid <- tibble(mtry = 1:5)

rf_rad_tuning <- map_dfr(rf_rad_grid$mtry, function(m) {
  rf_fit <- ranger(
    x = X_rad_train,
    y = y_train,
    num.trees = 20,
    mtry = m,
    min.node.size = 1,
    probability = TRUE,
    num.threads = 2
  )
  
  rf_prob <- predict(rf_fit, data = X_rad_test)$predictions[, "Cloud"]
  
  rf_pred <- ifelse(rf_prob > 0.5, "Cloud", "Not Cloud") %>%
    factor(levels = c("Not Cloud", "Cloud"))
  
  tibble(
    mtry = m,
    Accuracy = accuracy_vec(y_test, rf_pred),
    Sensitivity = sens_vec(y_test, rf_pred, event_level = "second"),
    AUROC = roc_auc_vec(y_test, rf_prob, event_level = "second")
  )
})

rf_rad_tuning

ggplot(rf_rad_tuning, aes(x=mtry, y = Accuracy)) +
  geom_line() + 
  geom_point() +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(title = "RF Tuning: Radiances", x = "mtry", y = "Accuracy")

best_mtry_rad <- rf_rad_tuning %>%
  slice_max(Accuracy, n = 1) %>%
  pull(mtry)

best_mtry_rad

rf_rad <- ranger(
  x = X_rad_train,
  y = y_train,
  num.trees = 20,
  mtry = best_mtry_rad,
  min.node.size = 1,
  probability = TRUE, 
  importance = "impurity"
)

rf_rad_prob <- predict(rf_rad, data = X_rad_test)$predictions[, "Cloud"]
rf_rad_pred <- ifelse(rf_rad_prob > 0.5, "Cloud", "Not Cloud") %>%
  factor(levels = c("Not Cloud", "Cloud"))

rf_rad_results <- evaluate_classification(
  y_true = y_test,
  y_pred = rf_rad_pred, 
  y_prob_pred = rf_rad_prob
)

rf_rad_results$metrics
rf_rad_results$confusion

```


## RF Engineered Features

For the engineered-feature random forest mode, the hyperparameter mtry was tuned over the values 1 through 3. The highest tuning accuracy was achieved at mtry = 1 so this value was selected for the final model. This shows the engineered-feature predictor set, allowing each split to consider a smaller number of predictors produced the strongest performance.

The final random forest model using engineered predictors achieved an accuracy of 0.918, sensitivity of 0.929, and AUROC of 0.971, demonstrating a strong classification performance. The engineered-feature model had a lower accuracy but higher sensitivity. This might suggest engineered-features are highly effective compared to the radiance-only random forest models. 

The results support the idea that the engineered features capture useful information for cloud detection.

```{r}
rf_eng_grid <- tibble(mtry = 1:3)
rf_eng_tuning <- map_dfr(rf_eng_grid$mtry, function(m){
  rf_fit <- ranger (
    x = X_eng_train,
    y = y_train,
    num.trees = 20,
    mtry = m,
    min.node.size = 1,
    probability = TRUE,
    num.threads = 2
  )
  
  
  rf_prob <- predict(rf_fit, data = X_eng_test)$predictions[, "Cloud"]
  
  rf_pred <- ifelse(rf_prob > 0.5, "Cloud", "Not Cloud")%>%
    factor(levels = c("Not Cloud", "Cloud"))
  
  tibble(
    mtry = m,
    Accuracy = accuracy_vec(y_test, rf_pred),
    Sensitivity = sens_vec(y_test, rf_pred, event_level = "second"),
    AUROC = roc_auc_vec(y_test, rf_prob, event_level = "second")
  )
  
})

rf_eng_tuning

ggplot(rf_eng_tuning, aes(x=mtry, y=Accuracy)) +
  geom_line() +
  geom_point() +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(title = "RF Tuning for Engineered Features", x = "mtry", y = "Accuracy")

best_mtry_eng <- rf_eng_tuning %>%
  slice_max(Accuracy, n = 1) %>%
  pull(mtry)

best_mtry_eng

rf_eng <- ranger(
  x = X_eng_train,
  y = y_train,
  num.trees = 100,
  mtry = best_mtry_eng,
  min.node.size = 1,
  probability = TRUE,
  importance = "impurity",
  num.threads = 2
)

rf_eng_prob <- predict(rf_eng, data = X_eng_test)$predictions[, "Cloud"]

rf_eng_pred <- ifelse(rf_eng_prob > 0.5, "Cloud", "Not Cloud") %>%
  factor(levels = c("Not Cloud", "Cloud"))

rf_eng_results <- evaluate_classification(
  y_true = y_test,
  y_pred = rf_eng_pred,
  y_prob_pred = rf_eng_prob
)

rf_eng_results$metrics
rf_eng_results$confusion



```


## Random Forest Model for All Predictors

For the random forest model using all predictors, mtry was tuned over the values 2,4,6, and 8. The highest tuning accuracy was achieved at mtry = 4, therefore selected for the final mode. The mtry = 2 produced slightly high sensitivity and AUROC but mtry = 4 gave the strongest overall accuracy. Sensitivity and AUROC were also reviewed.

The final model using all predictors achieved the strongest performance of all models with an accuracy of 0.958, sensitivity of 0.957, and AUROC of 0.991. The high sensitivity means the model correctly identifies most cloud pixels. High AUROC means a strong separation between cloud and non-cloud.

Radiance predictors with engineered features most likely provides the most complete information for cloud classification. The strong performance of the random forest model seemingly shows a nonlinear relationship between predicted and cloud labels. 

```{r}
rf_all_grid <- tibble(mtry=c(2,4,6,8))
rf_all_tuning <- map_dfr(rf_all_grid$mtry, function(m) {
  rf_fit <- ranger(
    x = X_all_train,
    y = y_train,
    num.trees = 20,
    mtry = m,
    min.node.size = 1,
    probability = TRUE,
    num.threads = 2
  )
  
  rf_prob <- predict(rf_fit, data = X_all_test)$predictions[, "Cloud"]
  
  rf_pred <- ifelse(rf_prob > 0.5, "Cloud", "Not Cloud") %>%
    factor(levels = c("Not Cloud", "Cloud"))
  
  tibble(
    mtry = m,
    Accuracy = accuracy_vec(y_test, rf_pred),
    Sensitivity = sens_vec(y_test, rf_pred, event_level = "second"),
    AUROC = roc_auc_vec(y_test, rf_prob, event_level = "second"),
  )
})

rf_all_tuning


```



```{r}
ggplot(rf_all_tuning, aes(x = mtry, y = Accuracy)) +
  geom_line() +
  geom_point() +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(
    title = "RF Tuning All Predictors",
    x = "mtry",
    y = "Accuracy"
  )


best_mtry_all <- rf_all_tuning %>%
  slice_max(Accuracy, n = 1, with_ties = FALSE) %>%
  pull(mtry)

best_mtry_all

rf_all <- ranger(
  x = X_all_train,
  y = y_train,
  num.trees = 20,
  mtry = best_mtry_all,
  min.node.size = 1,
  probability = TRUE,
  importance = "impurity",
  num.threads = 2
)

rf_all_prob <- predict(rf_all, data = X_all_test)$predictions[, "Cloud"]

rf_all_pred <- ifelse(rf_all_prob > 0.5, "Cloud", "Not Cloud") %>%
  factor(levels = c("Not Cloud", "Cloud"))

rf_all_results <- evaluate_classification(
  y_true = y_test,
  y_pred = rf_all_pred,
  y_prob_pred = rf_all_prob
)

rf_all_results$metrics
rf_all_results$confusion

```



## Model Comparison

To assess the fit of the models, three evaluation metrics were used. These included accuracy, sensitivity, and AUROC. 

Accuracy is a fundamental metric used for evaluating the performance of a classification model, giving the proportion of correct predictions made by the model out of all predictions.Sensitivity measures how many of the actual positive cases were correctly identified by the model. AUC-ROC helps understand how well a classification model distinguishes between two classes. In this case, it distinguishes cloud from non-cloud pixels across classification.

Among the models, it is clear that the random forest model performed much better than the LASSO model. For the LASSO models, radiance only performed the worst. Performance improved when using the engineered-features. The LASSO engineered-features showed the highest accuracy and sensitivity among the LASSO models. The LASSO all predictors model showed the highest AUROC (0.955).

All 3 random forest models were strong with the RF radiance achieving an accuracy of 0.926, sensitivity of 0.907, and AUROC of 0.973. The RF engineered model has a smaller lower accuracy but higher sensitivity. The RF all predictors model was the best out of the three with an accuracy of 0.959, sensitivity of 0.958, and AUROC of 0.991.

The best model was the random forest using all predictors with strong performances across the metrics.


```{r}
model_comparison <- bind_rows(
  lasso_rad_results$metrics %>% mutate(Model = "Lasso Radiances"),
  lasso_eng_results$metrics %>% mutate(Model = "Lasso Engineered"),
  lasso_all_results$metrics %>% mutate(Model = "Lasso All Predictors"),
  rf_rad_results$metrics %>% mutate(Model = "RF Radiances"),
  rf_eng_results$metrics %>% mutate(Model = "RF Engineered"),
  rf_all_results$metrics %>% mutate(Model = "RF All Predictors")
) %>%
  select(Model, everything())

model_comparison

```
